verses.text: So all Israel were reckoned by genealogies; and, behold, they were written in the book of the kings of Israel and Judah, who were carried away to Babylon for their transgression. Now the first inhabitants that dwelt in their possessions in their cities were, the Israelites, the priests, Levites, and the Nethinims. And in Jerusalem dwelt of the children of Judah, and of the children of Benjamin, and of the children of Ephraim, and Manasseh; Uthai the son of Ammihud, the son of Omri, the son of Imri, the son of Bani, of the children of Pharez the son of Judah. And of the Shilonites; Asaiah the firstborn, and his sons. And of the sons of Zerah; Jeuel, and their brethren, six hundred and ninety. And of the sons of Benjamin; Sallu the son of Meshullam, the son of Hodaviah, the son of Hasenuah, And Ibneiah the son of Jeroham, and Elah the son of Uzzi, the son of Michri, and Meshullam the son of Shephathiah, the son of Reuel, the son of Ibnijah; And their brethren, according to their generations, nine hundred and fifty and six. All these men were chief of the fathers in the house of their fathers. Guidance: The Chronicler now turns the corner from the long genealogies to the restored community, and he does so with painful honesty: all Israel was reckoned in the records, but they 'were carried away to Babylon for their transgression.' The exile is named for what it was — judgment for sin. Yet the very next breath speaks of restoration: 'the first inhabitants that dwelt in their possessions in their cities' after the return — Israelites, priests, Levites, and temple servants, with families of Judah, Benjamin, Ephraim, and Manasseh dwelling again in Jerusalem. The chapter is showing us the whole arc of the LORD's dealing with His people: sin, exile, and then return and resettlement. The genealogies that began with Adam have now arrived at the post-exilic remnant — the very readers of the book — re-established in their cities and in the holy city itself. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's judgments, however severe, are not His final word; the same God who carried His people to Babylon for their transgression brought a remnant home to dwell again in their possessions. The chapter is teaching us to be honest about sin and its consequences — the Chronicler does not soften the cause of the exile — yet also to take hold of the hope of restoration. The mention that representatives of Ephraim and Manasseh (northern tribes) dwelt in Jerusalem hints that the LORD was regathering not just Judah but a remnant of all Israel. The chapter is teaching the returned exiles that they were the living continuation of the ancient covenant people, replanted by grace in the land their fathers had lost — proof that the LORD remembers His own and brings them back from the far country.

And of the priests; Jedaiah, and Jehoiarib, and Jachin, And Azariah the son of Hilkiah, the son of Meshullam, the son of Zadok, the son of Meraioth, the son of Ahitub, the ruler of the house of God; And Adaiah the son of Jeroham, the son of Pashur, the son of Malchijah, and Maasiai the son of Adiel, the son of Jahzerah, the son of Meshullam, the son of Meshillemith, the son of Immer; And their brethren, heads of the house of their fathers, a thousand and seven hundred and threescore; very able men for the work of the service of the house of God. Guidance: Among the first to return and resettle were the priests — 1,760 of them, described as 'very able men for the work of the service of the house of God.' The Chronicler highlights that the restored community was, from the first, gathered around the temple and its ministry; the priests came back to take up again the service of the LORD's house. The chapter is showing us the priority of worship in the rebuilt nation: before kings or armies, the priests are named, the men set apart to serve at the altar and in the sanctuary. The chapter is teaching us that the service of God's house requires not only the right lineage but real ability — these were 'very able men for the work,' competent, capable, equipped for their sacred duties. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's work deserves and demands our best abilities; to serve in the house of God is a 'work,' a calling that calls for diligence and skill, not careless half-measures. The chapter is teaching the post-exilic community that the LORD had preserved a priesthood through the exile and brought back able men to lead them in worship — so that the broken connection with God's house could be restored. For us, it is a reminder that those who serve in the things of God should bring competence as well as consecration, ability joined to devotion. The restored temple needed able priests; the LORD's work always needs willing and capable servants who will give themselves to it wholeheartedly.

And of the Levites; Shemaiah the son of Hasshub, the son of Azrikam, the son of Hashabiah, of the sons of Merari; And Bakbakkar, Heresh, and Galal, and Mattaniah the son of Micah, the son of Zichri, the son of Asaph; And Obadiah the son of Shemaiah, the son of Galal, the son of Jeduthun, and Berechiah the son of Asa, the son of Elkanah, that dwelt in the villages of the Netophathites. Guidance: Alongside the priests, the Levites returned to take up their duties — and the Chronicler notes their descent from the great families of temple service, including Asaph and Jeduthun, the names associated with the temple singers David had appointed. The chapter is showing us the LORD's faithfulness in preserving the Levitical families through the exile, so that the full ministry of His house — not only the priestly sacrifices but the Levitical service and song — could be restored. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD provides for the whole work of His worship; He kept alive the families of singers and servants as well as priests, so that praise and ministry could resume in the rebuilt temple. The chapter is teaching us that every part of the service of God's house matters — the Levites who assisted, who sang, who kept the courts, were as needful to the restored worship as the priests at the altar. The note that some 'dwelt in the villages of the Netophathites' (near Bethlehem) shows that the Levites returned to their settlements scattered through the land, ready to come up and serve. The chapter is teaching the returned community that the LORD had not let the ministry of His house die in Babylon; the sons of Asaph and Jeduthun came home, and the songs of Zion would be sung again in Jerusalem. The God who preserves a people preserves also the means of their worship — He brings back not only the worshipers but those who lead them in worship.

And the porters were, Shallum, and Akkub, and Talmon, and Ahiman, and their brethren: Shallum was the chief; Who hitherto waited in the king's gate eastward: they were porters in the companies of the children of Levi. And Shallum the son of Kore, the son of Ebiasaph, the son of Korah, and his brethren, of the house of his father, the Korahites, were over the work of the service, keepers of the gates of the tabernacle: and their fathers, being over the host of the LORD, were keepers of the entry. And Phinehas the son of Eleazar was the ruler over them in time past, and the LORD was with him. And Zechariah the son of Meshelemiah was porter of the door of the tabernacle of the congregation. All these which were chosen to be porters in the gates were two hundred and twelve. These were reckoned by their genealogy in their villages, whom David and Samuel the seer did ordain in their set office. So they and their children had the oversight of the gates of the house of the LORD, namely, the house of the tabernacle, by wards. In four quarters were the porters, toward the east, west, north, and south. And their brethren, which were in their villages, were to come after seven days from time to time with them. For these Levites, the four chief porters, were in their set office, and were over the chambers and treasuries of the house of God. And they lodged round about the house of God, because the charge was upon them, and the opening thereof every morning pertained to them. Guidance: The Chronicler gives careful, even loving, attention to the porters — the gatekeepers of the house of the LORD. Far from a menial afterthought, this was an honoured office: their service traced back to faithful Phinehas, 'and the LORD was with him'; their stations were 'ordain[ed]' by 'David and Samuel the seer'; they kept the gates in all four quarters, guarded the chambers and treasuries, lodged around the house of God because 'the charge was upon them,' and to them 'the opening thereof every morning pertained.' The chapter is showing us the dignity and trust of even the humblest-seeming temple work. The gatekeeper stood at the threshold of God's house, guarding its holiness, controlling who and what came in, opening it faithfully each new morning. The chapter is teaching us that there is great honour in keeping the door of God's house — 'I had rather be a doorkeeper in the house of my God, than to dwell in the tents of wickedness' (Ps 84:10, written by these very sons of Korah). The chapter is teaching us that faithfulness, trustworthiness, and watchfulness are precious in the LORD's service; the porters were entrusted with the treasuries and the daily opening of the house because they could be relied upon. The chapter is teaching us that no station in God's service is small if it is held faithfully — the man at the gate, the keeper of the keys, the one who opens the house each morning, does holy and necessary work. And the note that 'the LORD was with' their forefather Phinehas reminds us that the LORD's presence is the true reward of faithful service in any office. The chapter is teaching the post-exilic community to value every role in the restored worship, from the priest at the altar to the porter at the gate, for all alike serve the house of God.

And they lodged round about the house of God, because the charge was upon them, and the opening thereof every morning pertained to them. Guidance: The Chronicler gives careful, even loving, attention to the porters — the gatekeepers of the house of the LORD. Far from a menial afterthought, this was an honoured office: their service traced back to faithful Phinehas, 'and the LORD was with him'; their stations were 'ordain[ed]' by 'David and Samuel the seer'; they kept the gates in all four quarters, guarded the chambers and treasuries, lodged around the house of God because 'the charge was upon them,' and to them 'the opening thereof every morning pertained.' The chapter is showing us the dignity and trust of even the humblest-seeming temple work. The gatekeeper stood at the threshold of God's house, guarding its holiness, controlling who and what came in, opening it faithfully each new morning. The chapter is teaching us that there is great honour in keeping the door of God's house — 'I had rather be a doorkeeper in the house of my God, than to dwell in the tents of wickedness' (Ps 84:10, written by these very sons of Korah). The chapter is teaching us that faithfulness, trustworthiness, and watchfulness are precious in the LORD's service; the porters were entrusted with the treasuries and the daily opening of the house because they could be relied upon. The chapter is teaching us that no station in God's service is small if it is held faithfully — the man at the gate, the keeper of the keys, the one who opens the house each morning, does holy and necessary work. And the note that 'the LORD was with' their forefather Phinehas reminds us that the LORD's presence is the true reward of faithful service in any office. The chapter is teaching the post-exilic community to value every role in the restored worship, from the priest at the altar to the porter at the gate, for all alike serve the house of God.

And the porters were, Shallum, and Akkub, and Talmon, and Ahiman, and their brethren: Shallum was the chief; Who hitherto waited in the king's gate eastward: they were porters in the companies of the children of Levi. And Shallum the son of Kore, the son of Ebiasaph, the son of Korah, and his brethren, of the house of his father, the Korahites, were over the work of the service, keepers of the gates of the tabernacle: and their fathers, being over the host of the LORD, were keepers of the entry. And Phinehas the son of Eleazar was the ruler over them in time past, and the LORD was with him. And Zechariah the son of Meshelemiah was porter of the door of the tabernacle of the congregation. All these which were chosen to be porters in the gates were two hundred and twelve. These were reckoned by their genealogy in their villages, whom David and Samuel the seer did ordain in their set office. So they and their children had the oversight of the gates of the house of the LORD, namely, the house of the tabernacle, by wards. And certain of them had the charge of the ministering vessels, that they should bring them in and out by tale. Some of them also were appointed to oversee the vessels, and all the instruments of the sanctuary, and the fine flour, and the wine, and the oil, and the frankincense, and the spices. And some of the sons of the priests made the ointment of the spices. And Mattithiah, one of the Levites, who was the firstborn of Shallum the Korahite, had the set office over the things that were made in the pans. And other of their brethren, of the sons of the Kohathites, were over the shewbread, to prepare it every sabbath. And these are the singers, chief of the fathers of the Levites, who remaining in the chambers were free: for they were employed in that work day and night. These chief fathers of the Levites were chief throughout their generations; these dwelt at Jerusalem. Guidance: The Chronicler details the many particular charges entrusted to the Levites: counting the ministering vessels in and out 'by tale,' overseeing the instruments of the sanctuary and the fine flour, wine, oil, frankincense, and spices; making the holy ointment; baking 'the things that were made in the pans'; preparing the shewbread every sabbath; and the singers, who 'were employed in that work day and night.' Every detail of the temple's provision had its appointed keeper. The chapter is showing us the careful, orderly division of the LORD's service — nothing left to chance, every sacred vessel accounted for, every element of worship in faithful hands. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD is a God of order, not confusion, and that His worship is to be conducted with care and reverence down to the smallest detail. The vessels were counted in and out; the shewbread was prepared faithfully every sabbath; the singers praised day and night. The chapter is teaching us that faithfulness in small and unglamorous tasks — counting cups, grinding spices, baking bread, guarding oil — is genuine service to God when done for His house. The chapter is teaching us, in the singers 'employed in that work day and night,' the beauty of continual praise: some were set apart so that the worship of God might never cease. The chapter is teaching the restored community, and us, that the service of the LORD embraces a thousand humble duties, and that those who keep the vessels and prepare the bread and sing through the night are all alike ministers of God, each doing holy work in his appointed place. Order, faithfulness, and unceasing praise — these marked the house of the LORD, and these are to mark the worship of His people still.

And in Gibeon dwelt the father of Gibeon, Jehiel, whose wife's name was Maachah: And his firstborn son Abdon, then Zur, and Kish, and Baal, and Ner, and Nadab, And Gedor, and Ahio, and Zechariah, and Mikloth. And Mikloth begat Shimeam. And they also dwelt with their brethren at Jerusalem, over against their brethren. And Ner begat Kish; and Kish begat Saul; and Saul begat Jonathan, and Malchishua, and Abinadab, and Eshbaal. And the son of Jonathan was Meribbaal: and Meribbaal begat Micah. And the sons of Micah were, Pithon, and Melech, and Tahrea, and Ahaz. And Ahaz begat Jarah; and Jarah begat Alemeth, and Azmaveth, and Zimri; and Zimri begat Moza; And Moza begat Binea; and Rephaiah his son, Eleasah his son, Azel his son. And Azel had six sons, whose names are these, Azrikam, Bocheru, and Ishmael, and Sheariah, and Obadiah, and Hanan: these were the sons of Azel. Guidance: The Chronicler closes the long genealogical section by returning to the family of Gibeon and the line of Saul — Ner, Kish, Saul, Jonathan, and their descendants. This repetition (the line appeared also in chapter 8) is deliberate: it forms the bridge from the genealogies into the narrative that now begins, for the very next chapter will tell of Saul's death and the passing of the kingdom to David. The chapter is showing us the Chronicler turning from the static record of names to the living history of Israel's kings, and he chooses to begin where the monarchy began — with Saul. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD orders the transitions of His story; having established who the people are through nine chapters of genealogy, the Chronicler now sets the stage for the great theme of his book: the kingdom of David and the worship of God. The chapter is teaching us that Saul's house, soon to fall, is placed here at the hinge of the book as the dark background against which David's God-given kingship will shine. The repeated names of Saul's line remind us that the throne once rested with Benjamin, but was about to pass, by the LORD's decree, to Judah and David. The chapter is teaching us that all the careful genealogies have been leading to this: the unfolding of the LORD's purpose to raise up David and, through David's house, to establish a kingdom and a temple that point to the everlasting King. As the names of Saul's descendants trail off, the reader senses that one chapter of Israel's story is closing and another, the story of David, is about to open — under the sovereign hand of the God who turns the kingdom according to His will.
